title: Controlling Code Execution in the Debugger
breadcrumbs: Controlling Code Execution in the Debugger
text: Controlling Code Execution in the Debugger Debugger execution control commands allow the application to run through ProcScript that is not of interest (under varying degrees of control), and then return control to the Debugger. The Debugger runs at the same time as the application that is being debugged. However, at any given moment, the execution of ProcScript is under the control of either the Debugger or the application, not both. Even while an application is running under Debugger control, the application still reacts to actions of the user interface. Debugger Focus and Control At the beginning of a debugging session, the Debugger has control and focus. Control and focus switch to the application under the following circumstances: The application reaches an idle state, waiting for user action. A nodebug ProcScript statement is encountered. The Go command is issued. Control and focus switch from the application to the Debugger whenever: A breakpoint is reached. A debug ProcScript statement is encountered. (When the nodebug statement is encountered, this is the only way the Debugger can regain control). The Debug >Halt command is issued. Execution Control Commands Use the following commands, available from the toolbar and the Debug menu, to control ProcScript execution. Table 1. Debugger Execution Control Commands Toolbar Icon Command Action Go Switches control to the application, allowing it to run until control is returned to the Debugger. Step Into The current ProcScript statement is executed, then control is returned to the Debugger. Step Over Steps over invocation ProcScript statements such as call and activate , then returns control to the Debugger after the invocation. This behavior also applies to the implicit calls of the read statement. Step Out Directs the application to run until the current ProcScript module returns or ends. The return is the actual end of the module, even if there is not an explicit return statement. If another breakpoint or debug statement occurs before the return, the application breaks there first. Run to Cursor Passes control to the application until the selected ProcScript statement is reached; it acts as a temporary breakpoint. Highlight the line of code in the Module pane that you want to act as the temporary breakpoint, then choose Debug > Run to Cursor . The application runs until it reaches the highlighted line of code, that becomes the new current ProcScript statement. Skip Causes the current ProcScript statement to be skipped. It is not possible to skip the done ProcScript statement at the end of a module. Halt Forces a running application to break immediately, returning control to the Debugger. When the application has control, the Go , Step Into , Step Over , Step Out , Run to Cursor , and Skip menu items are dimmed. When the Debugger has control, the Halt menu item is dimmed. Related Links Breakpoints A breakpoint is a marked code line, trigger, error, exception, or condition that forces the application or component to break and the debugger to take control of execution. Monitoring ProcScript Modules The Uniface Debugger allows you to monitor and control the execution of ProcScript. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
